command: bash -lc 'ufw --force enable && ufw default deny incoming && ufw logging high && iptables -I ufw-before-input 1 -p udp --dport 7001 -m state --state NEW -m recent --name S1 --set && iptables -I ufw-before-input 1 -p tcp --dport 8001 -m tcp --syn -m recent --rcheck --seconds 10 --name S1 -m recent --name S2 --set && iptables -I ufw-before-input 1 -p udp --dport 9001 -m state --state NEW -m recent --rcheck --seconds 10 --name S2 -m recent --name S3 --set && iptables -I ufw-before-input 1 -p tcp --dport 2222 -m recent --rcheck --seconds 20 --name S3 -j ACCEPT'
explanation: Implements a mixed-protocol knock: UDP/7001, then TCP SYN to 8001, then UDP/9001. Only after this ordered sequence does TCP/2222 open briefly to the source. This is classic T1205 signaling using a multi-step sequence across protocols. Detection: Look for the precise ordered triplet of connection attempts from the same IP and a short-lived acceptance on TCP/2222.
rationale_steps: Enable ufw and deny incoming by default; enable logging.
On TCP SYN to 8001 with S1 set, set S2.
On UDP/9001 with S2 set, set S3.
Accept TCP/2222 if S3 is present within 20 seconds.
Detect by correlating the specific 7001→8001(SYN)→9001 pattern in ufw/pcap logs.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Balanced quoting with proper && chaining and valid ufw/iptables/recent/state flags, including permissible match-only iptables rules with side effects.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: Accurately implements a multi-step port-knock sequence using iptables recent lists in the UFW chain to briefly allow TCP/2222 for the knocking source, but it requires root privileges (contradicting 'Privilege: None') and depends on UFW/iptables backend presence.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed with balanced quoting, proper use of && separators, valid iptables flags and options, and no syntax errors throughout the entire chain.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command correctly implements a multi-protocol port-knocking sequence (UDP/7001 → TCP SYN/8001 → UDP/9001 → TCP/2222 accept) using iptables recent module with named state tracking (S1, S2, S3) and time-bounded checks, which is a textbook T1205 traffic signaling implementation for covert access triggering.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is a perfectly well-formed bash string using proper logic operators (&&), balanced quotes, and correct syntax for both the UFW and iptables utilities.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command precisely implements the described multi-stage port knocking sequence using the iptables 'recent' module to track state across different protocols and ports, which is a classic implementation of T1205.
